perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such breach may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and not as a penalty) in respect of any such breach, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $20,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a breach of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such breach may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and also as a penalty) in respect of any such breach, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $20,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the breach.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies liquidated damages are 'not a penalty.' The modified text contradicts this by stating they are 'also a penalty.'
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356 states that damages for breach may be liquidated in the agreement but only at an amount that is reasonable in light of the anticipated or actual loss caused by the breach and the difficulties of proof of loss. A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is unenforceable on public policy as a penalty. Claiming it as both liquidated damage AND penalty introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the clause.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly contradicts the established legal principle that liquidated damages cannot also be a penalty. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that the original text specifies that liquidated damages are 'not a penalty,' while the modified text contradicts this by stating they are 'also a penalty.' The Law Explanation reinforces this by noting that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356 deems unreasonably large liquidated damages unenforceable as a penalty, and that claiming liquidated damages as BOTH liquidated damage AND penalty introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the clause. The scraped snippets do not provide additional legal context, but the other supplied information indicates that classifying liquidated damages also as a penalty creates a legal contradiction, as true liquidated damages are meant to be a fair estimate of damages, not a punitive measure. Therefore, the modification introduces a direct conflict with legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: From and after the date hereof, the Undersigneds will not utilize the name "The World Golf Tour," or any confusingly similar name in connection with any business activities from and after the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: From and after the date hereof, the Undersigneds will not utilize the name "The World Golf Tour," or any somewhat related name in connection with any business activities from and after the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "confusingly similar" to "somewhat related" broadens the scope of the restriction beyond what is legally enforceable under trademark law. 'Confusingly similar' has a specific legal definition within trademark law, while 'somewhat related' is vague and overbroad.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act protects against the use of marks that are likely to cause confusion, mistake, or deception. The standard is 'likelihood of confusion,' not merely being 'somewhat related.' This broadens the scope beyond legal protection, making the clause potentially unenforceable to the extent it restricts non-confusing uses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. The United States, all agencies and instrume
perturbation.justification: The change from 'confusingly similar' to 'somewhat related' broadens the scope of the restriction beyond what is legally enforceable under trademark law. The Lanham Act, as supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, focuses on preventing uses that are 'likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive.' The original text, using 'confusingly similar,' aligns with this standard. However, 'somewhat related' is a much broader term, potentially restricting uses that do not cause confusion and are therefore not protected by trademark law. This creates a legal contradiction because the modified text seeks to enforce restrictions beyond the scope of the Lanham Act, potentially making it unenforceable. The LLM explanation correctly points out that the legal standard is 'likelihood of confusion,' not merely being 'somewhat related,' solidifying the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented --------- only by an instrument in writing signed by the parties to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by an instrument in writing signed by at least one of the parties to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires all parties to sign amendments. The changed text requires only one, contradicting the principle of mutual agreement in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Principles of Contract Law - Mutual Assent
perturbation.law_explanation: Mutual assent is a fundamental requirement for contract formation and modification. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 17 requires a 'bargain in which there is a manifestation of mutual assent to the exchange and a consideration.' Allowing amendment with only one party's signature contradicts this principle, making such amendment potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: offer | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you offer Offer is part of contract negotiations where a party agrees to do or not do something in exchange for consideration . An offer must be stated and delivered in a way that would lead a reasonable person to expect a binding contract to arise from its acceptance . An offer gives the offeree the ability to accept and form an enforceable contract. When a valid offer is made, an acceptance by the offeree creates a binding contract. An offeror generally cannot retract the offer once the other party has accepted. A court may not find a binding contract regardless, though, if the negotiation did not involve consideration, involves an illusory promise , or if the offeror did not have the capacity to enter into the contract. However, a contract offer can be withdrawn by notification to the offeree of the intent to withdraw the offer before the offeree has accepted. Offerors typically can withdraw an offer before acceptance in any contract negotiation unless the parties have created an enforceable option contract . If the offeree rejects the offer, the offer can no longer be accepted by the offeree. An offer can also be made in the form of a counteroffer . The offeree that receives the initial offer can make a new offer of their own instead of accepting the initial offer. This counteroffer acts as a rejection of the initial offer and creates an entirely new offer that can be accepted or rejected. [Last reviewed in August of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the requirement of mutual assent in contract law. The original text mandates that all parties must sign any amendments, ensuring mutual agreement. By contrast, the changed text allows amendments to be made with the signature of only one party, undermining the principle that all parties must agree to changes in the contract. The Law Explanation highlights that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 17 requires a 'manifestation of mutual assent,' a condition not met when only one party's signature is sufficient for amendment. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that "An offer must be stated and delivered in a way that would lead a reasonable person to expect a binding contract to arise from its acceptance". By changing the agreement so that only one party needs to agree to the terms, this undermines the original agreement that all parties needed to agree to amendments. This shift directly opposes the fundamental requirement of mutual agreement, thus creating a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be submitted for mediation in Salt Lake City, Utah. In the event mediation is unsuccessful, the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of an appropriate court within Salt Lake County, State of Utah, to hear and decide any controversy, claim, or dispute hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be subject to mandatory arbitration, at the sole discretion of the Purchaser, in a location of the Purchaser's choosing. The arbitrator's decision shall be final and binding, with no right to appeal.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies mediation followed by court jurisdiction. The modified text mandates arbitration at the Purchaser's discretion, eliminates court access, and removes the right to appeal.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act generally favors arbitration, contracts of adhesion with unconscionable terms (like mandating arbitration at one party's sole discretion and denying appeal rights) can be challenged. The changed text creates an imbalance of power that could render the arbitration clause unenforceable, contradicting the general intent of the FAA to enforce valid arbitration agreements. The 'location of the Purchaser's choosing' may also be considered unconscionable if it places undue burden on other parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original agreement by mandating arbitration at the Purchaser's sole discretion, eliminating court access, and removing the right to appeal. This creates an imbalance of power and could render the arbitration clause unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 U.S. Code § 2, states that arbitration agreements are valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, "save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract." The Law Explanation argues that terms like 'location of the Purchaser's choosing' could be deemed unconscionable, potentially making the agreement revocable under existing law. The change from mediation followed by court jurisdiction to mandatory arbitration, particularly with the purchaser's discretion and no right to appeal, represents a significant shift that could be considered unconscionable, creating a legal contradiction.
